Ownership Rights
Lesson 1: Supplemental Benefits
Learning Objective
Identify three types of supplemental disability benefits that can be added to
life insurance policies.
Open Transcript
Life Insurance
Life insurance policies protect millions of lives and help meet a variety of
financial needs.
But sometimes the basic insurance policy is not a complete solution.
Supplemental Benefits

**[CHART]**

payload.summary: LOMA 281 Module 3 Lesson 1 Supplemental Benefits Summary of Key Points Life insurance policyowners can purchase several different types of supplemental benefits to add to their policies. Supplemental disability benefits include: • waiver of premium for disability (WP)— the insurer waives renewal premiums while the insured is totally disabled • waiver of premium for payor—the insurer waives renewal premiums if the policyowner dies or becomes totally disabled • disability income benefit—provides a monthly income benefit to the policyowner- insured if he becomes totally disabled Accident-related benefits include: • accidental death benefit—provides an additional death benefit if an accident causes the insured’s death • accidental death and dismemberment (AD&D) benefit—provides an additional benefit if an accident causes the insured to die or to lose any two limbs or the sight in both eyes Accelerated death benefits include: • terminal illness (TI) benefit—pays part of the death benefit to a policyowner-insured with a terminal illness • dread disease (DD) benefit—pays part of the death benefit to the policyowner if the insured suffers from a specified disease • long-term care (LTC) insurance benefit—pays a monthly benefit to the policyowner if the insured requires constant care for a medical condition Coverage for additional insureds includes: • spouse and children’s insurance rider • children’s insurance rider • second insured rider Insurability benefit riders—an individual can purchase insurance without new evidence of insurability—include: • guaranteed insurability (GI) benefit—the policyowner can purchase additional insurance on stated dates • paid-up additions option benefit—(for whole life only) the policyowner can purchase single-premium paid-up additions on stated dates Copyright © 2020 LOMA (Life Office Management Association, Inc.). All rights reserved.

Course: LOMA 281 - Meeting Customer Needs with Insurance and Annuities - 3rd Edition | Module: Module 3 - Benefits, Provisions, and Ownership Rights | Lesson: Lesson 1 - Supplemental Benefits | Source name: LOMA281_M3L1_Knowledge File_Supplemental Benefits | Definition: A rider that waives the policy's premiums if the insured becomes totally disabled after a waiting period, keeping the policy in force. | Domain Tags: disability, waiver of premium, insured | Related Nodes: KN-002 | Category: Rider Type | Node Name: Waiver of Premium for Disability (WP) | Chunk Text Content: Under a WP benefit, the insurance company pays the renewal premiums if
the insured becomes totally disabled and satisfies a three- to six-month
waiting period.
In other words, the insured must be totally disabled for three to six months
and continue to make the premium payments that come due before the
insurer will begin waiving premium payments. Some policies will refund the
premiums paid during the waiting period if the insured is still disabled
when the waiting period ends.

the duties of any occupation for which the insured is reasonably suited by
education, training, or experience. The insurer usually requires periodic
proof that the insured continues to be totally disabled.

Under a waiver of premium for payor benefit, the insurer
waives renewal premiums if the policyowner, rather than the insured, dies
or becomes totally disabled.
For example, the benefit often is attached to a juvenile insurance policy
covering the life of a child. The policy is owned and paid for by an adult,
usually the child’s parent or legal guardian.

Accidental Death and Dismemberment (AD&D)
Benefit
The accidental death and dismemberment (AD&D) benefit pays an
accidental death benefit if an insured dies by accidental means or it pays a
dismemberment benefit to an insured if an accident causes the insured to
lose any two limbs or the sight in both eyes.

Accelerated Death Benefit Riders
Learning Objective
Identify three types of accelerated death benefit riders and recognize the
differences among those riders.
Accelerated Death Benefits (Living Benefits)
An accelerated death benefit (living benefit) allows a policyowner access to
some or all of a life insurance policy’s death benefit in certain circumstances
while the insured is alive.

(TI) benefit, which pays a portion of the policy’s death benefit if the insured
has a terminal illness with a physician-certified life expectancy of less than
a specified period, usually 12 or 24 months.
Although TI benefits are commonly used to pay for medical care, living
expenses, or outstanding debts, they can be used for any purpose, such as
travel expenses.

a spouse or child. What do you think is the normal limit for coverage units?

5 to 10

10 to 20

20 to 40
Answer: 5 to 10
